heroOverview: Scabies is a highly contagious skin infestation caused by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis, which burrows into the outer layer of the epidermis to lay eggs. Worldwide, an estimated 200 million people have scabies at any given time, making it one of the most prevalent skin conditions on earth. The cardinal symptom is intense generalised itching, worst at night, caused not by the mite itself but by the immune system's delayed hypersensitivity reaction to mite proteins, faeces, and egg components. Scabies spreads readily within households, care homes, and any setting involving prolonged skin-to-skin contact. Without treatment the infestation persists indefinitely, but a single properly applied course of permethrin cream or a single oral dose of ivermectin is curative in most cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 200 million active cases globally at any one time; 300 million new episodes annually (WHO, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: All ages and social groups affected; disproportionate burden in low- and middle-income tropical regions and crowded institutional settings
keyStats.avgAge: Two peaks: young children under 5 and older adults in care facilities; sexually active adults also a high-incidence group
keyStats.globalCases: Recognised as a neglected tropical disease by WHO since 2017; ~300 million episodes per year worldwide

definition: Scabies (ICD-10: B86) is a parasitic skin infestation caused by the obligate ectoparasite Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis, a microscopic arachnid mite. The fertilised female mite measures approximately 0.3–0.4 mm and burrows into the stratum corneum — the outermost epidermal layer — where she lays 2–3 eggs per day for her 4–6 week lifespan. Eggs hatch in 3–4 days and larvae reach reproductive maturity in a further 10–15 days, sustaining and expanding the infestation. The average number of mites in classic scabies is small — approximately 10–15 per host — and the profound pruritus and rash are the result of a delayed Type IV hypersensitivity response to mite antigens that develops over 4–6 weeks in a first-time infestation; previously sensitised individuals can react within 24–48 hours of re-exposure. Scabies falls under infectious disease and dermatology. The WHO recognises it as a neglected tropical disease and a priority health issue. A severe hyperinfestive variant, crusted (Norwegian) scabies, occurs in immunocompromised or elderly individuals and involves millions of mites; it is highly contagious and requires more aggressive treatment. Scabies is distributed worldwide but disproportionately affects populations in overcrowded, resource-poor settings.

typesClassification.description: The typical form with a small mite burden (10–15 mites), producing intensely pruritic papules, vesicles, and burrows at characteristic body sites; contagious but not exceptionally so.

A hyperinfestation variant with millions of mites, occurring in immunocompromised, elderly, or neurologically impaired hosts; the skin is covered in thick grey-white crusts, itching may be mild, and the patient is extremely infectious.

A post-scabetic inflammatory reaction producing persistent itchy brown or red nodules on the groin, scrotum, or axillae that can persist weeks to months after the mites are eradicated, representing prolonged immune hypersensitivity.

In children under two, the distribution is atypical — involving the scalp, face, palms, and soles — and blisters and pustules are more common than in adults; secondary bacterial infection is a frequent complication.
A modified presentation in which previous partial treatment or regular moisturiser use reduces visible rash while mites persist; diagnosis is easily missed because burrows are absent or few.

primarySymptoms: Intense generalised pruritus, dramatically worse at night, that typically begins 4–6 weeks after first infestation when the sensitising immune response develops; subsequent infestations trigger itching within 24–48 hours.

Burrows — thin, grey-white, thread-like tracks 5–15 mm long in the skin surface — representing the actual tunnels excavated by the female mite; most reliably found in the finger webs, wrists, and genitals.
Erythematous papules and vesicles at the burrow sites and at other body locations affected by the hypersensitivity response, appearing as small red bumps that may be excoriated from scratching.
A generalised maculopapular or eczematous rash extending beyond the mite sites as the immune response amplifies, often covering the trunk, thighs, and buttocks.
Excoriation marks — linear scratch marks in the skin — resulting from the constant urge to scratch, which can obscure the primary lesions and create entry points for bacteria.

Characteristic distribution pattern involving the finger webs, wrists, inner forearms, axillae, around the nipples, periumbilical area, lower abdomen, genitalia, buttocks, and inner thighs — with relative sparing of the face and scalp in adults.

Genital involvement with papules or nodules on the penis, scrotum, or vulva; genital involvement is highly characteristic of scabies and alerts the clinician to consider the diagnosis even when the overall picture is unclear.
Secondary bacterial infection — impetigo, folliculitis, or frank cellulitis — visible as crusted golden-yellow lesions, increased warmth, and swelling at heavily scratched sites.
Thick, hyperkeratotic, greyish crusts covering large body surface areas in crusted scabies, sometimes with absent or paradoxically mild pruritus due to immune dysfunction.
Persistent itchy brown or reddish nodules in the groin, axillae, or genital area in nodular scabies, persisting weeks after successful mite eradication.
earlyWarningSigns: Increasing nighttime itching that disturbs sleep, especially if a close contact has recently been similarly affected.
Itching disproportionate to any visible rash — characteristic of the early sensitisation phase before the full rash develops.
Subtle thread-like marks in the skin of the finger webs noticed before significant rash appears.
New itchy lesions on the genitals in a context where other skin conditions would not be expected.
emergencySigns: Signs of sepsis — fever, rapid pulse, low blood pressure, or confusion — with widespread skin infection, which can follow secondary bacterial infection of extensive scabies and is a medical emergency.
Rapidly spreading redness, warmth, and swelling of a limb or large body area indicating spreading cellulitis requiring urgent antibiotics.
Crusted scabies with respiratory compromise or systemic illness in a severely immunocompromised patient — requires hospitalisation and aggressive combined treatment.

causes.cause: Direct prolonged skin-to-skin contact with an infested person
causes.description: The primary route of transmission; the scabies mite cannot jump or fly and transfers only through sustained close physical contact of at least several minutes — as occurs during sexual contact, holding hands, or sleeping in the same bed — because it can only crawl slowly across skin.

Genital-to-body skin contact during sexual activity is one of the most efficient transmission routes because it involves prolonged mutual skin exposure; scabies is classified as an STI in the context of sexual transmission, though it can spread by any close contact route.

Sharing a bed or living space with an infested person creates multiple daily opportunities for prolonged contact; scabies spreads rapidly through households, care homes, military barracks, prisons, and other crowded residential settings where untreated cohabitants serve as a reservoir.

causes.cause: Fomite transmission via infested bedding and clothing
causes.description: The female mite survives off the host for 24–36 hours at room temperature; while fomite transmission is considered a secondary route in classic scabies, it is a significant contributor in crusted scabies where vast numbers of shed mites contaminate bedding, clothing, and furniture.

Failure of normal immune-mediated mite control in HIV/AIDS, haematological malignancy, corticosteroid therapy, organ transplantation, or very old age allows unchecked mite reproduction to produce crusted scabies, which then becomes an explosive community transmission source.

riskFactors.description: Overcrowded households, care homes, prisons, military barracks, refugee camps, and boarding schools provide repeated opportunities for prolonged skin contact and make household eradication difficult; endemic scabies cycles persist in these settings.

riskFactors.factor: Sexual activity and close physical contact
riskFactors.description: Frequent sexual contact with multiple partners increases the probability of contact with an infested individual; scabies transmission during sexual activity is efficient because of the prolonged skin-to-skin exposure involved.
Young children have more frequent close physical contact with caregivers and peers, atypical distribution affecting face and scalp, and a higher risk of secondary bacterial infection.

Elderly adults in residential care homes face high risk from close care-giving contact; cognitive impairment may prevent them from reporting itching, and immunosenescence predisposes to crusted scabies.

HIV infection, haematological malignancy, systemic corticosteroid use, organ transplantation, and functional asplenia all impair the normal cellular immune response that limits mite proliferation, dramatically increasing crusted scabies risk.

Prior sensitisation means that symptoms develop rapidly — within 24–48 hours — after re-exposure rather than the 4–6 week delay of first infestation; individuals in endemic settings are at elevated re-infestation risk.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to endemic tropical regions
riskFactors.description: Hot, humid climates favour mite survival off the host; scabies prevalence reaches 25–45% in some Pacific Island and sub-Saharan African communities, making exposure risk very high for travellers and long-term residents.

riskFactors.factor: Failure of household contacts to receive simultaneous treatment
riskFactors.description: The single greatest risk factor for recurrence; untreated household members re-infest treated individuals almost inevitably, explaining why whole-household treatment is mandated by every major guideline.

affectedDemographics: Children under five years, in whom scalp and face involvement is typical
Older adults in residential care homes, in whom crusted scabies risk is highest
Sexually active adults with multiple partners
Residents of overcrowded low-income housing or institutional settings
Immunocompromised individuals across all age groups
All household members living with an index case, regardless of symptoms
diagnosisOverview: Scabies is primarily a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic symptom triad of nocturnal pruritus, burrows at typical sites (finger webs, wrists, genitalia), and the same symptoms in a close contact. The presence of all three elements is highly specific, though in real-world practice the full triad is present in only a minority of cases. Dermatoscopy has transformed bedside diagnosis: the 'delta-wing jet' or 'hang-glider' sign — the triangular dark brown head of the mite at the end of a burrow — is detectable in 83–97% of authentic burrows under dermoscopy and allows confident diagnosis without laboratory testing. Skin scraping of a burrow with mineral oil, examined under a microscope for mites, eggs, or faeces, confirms the diagnosis in 40–70% of classic scabies cases — sensitivity is limited by the small mite burden and sampling variation. Burrow ink test: felt-tip ink applied to skin and wiped off, leaving ink in burrow tracks, is a cheap bedside adjunct. In crusted scabies, microscopy of skin scrapings yields high mite densities and is more reliably positive. Molecular PCR testing of skin scrapings can identify mite DNA and is used in research settings. The differential diagnosis includes eczema, contact dermatitis, urticaria, psoriasis, impetigo, and bullous pemphigoid in older adults. A key clinical principle is that itching may persist for up to four to six weeks after successful mite eradication (post-scabetic pruritus) due to residual immune sensitisation; this must be distinguished from treatment failure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive bedside visualisation of the scabies mite at the end of a burrow as the 'delta-wing' sign, providing rapid and accurate diagnosis with sensitivity above 80%.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small handheld magnifying device with a light is placed against the skin where a burrow is suspected; painless and takes only minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the classic thin, grey-white, thread-like tracks of mite burrows in the finger webs, wrists, and genitalia that distinguish scabies from other pruritic dermatoses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines the skin in good light, focusing on typical sites; may use a magnifying glass or oblique illumination.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis by identifying mites, eggs, or faecal pellets under light microscopy after scraping the top of a burrow onto a glass slide with mineral oil.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blunt scalpel is used to scrape the skin superficially — a mildly uncomfortable but not painful procedure; the sample is examined in the laboratory within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Makes burrows visible to the naked eye by applying felt-tip pen ink to the skin surface, rubbing off excess, and identifying retained ink tracks corresponding to burrows.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ink is applied and wiped off in seconds; a positive test confirms the presence of burrows and supports the scabies diagnosis without laboratory equipment.

diagnosticTests.test: Full STI screen (in adults presenting through sexual health services)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Scabies can be sexually transmitted; co-infections with chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, and HIV should be excluded when the likely route is sexual contact.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard urogenital swab or urine test for bacterial STIs and a blood draw for serological tests; results available within 24–72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Bacterial wound swab of secondarily infected lesions
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies causative organisms of secondary bacterial infection — most often Streptococcus pyogenes or Staphylococcus aureus — to guide antibiotic selection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cotton swab is wiped across a crusted or oozing area; laboratory culture results take 24–48 hours.

treatmentOverview: The goal of treatment is to kill all mites and viable eggs with a topical or systemic scabicidal agent while simultaneously treating all household and close contacts to prevent re-infestation. Permethrin 5% cream is the first-line topical treatment in most international guidelines including WHO, CDC, NICE, and ACNE/BAD: it is applied from the neck down to the soles (including under fingernails, in skin folds, and in the genital area), left on for 8–12 hours, and washed off; the application is repeated seven days later to catch any newly hatched larvae from surviving eggs. In infants and young children, the scalp, face, and ears must also be treated. Oral ivermectin (200 mcg/kg as a single dose, repeated at day 8–15) is an equivalent first-line alternative recommended by WHO for community mass treatment campaigns and preferred when topical application is impractical; it is the treatment of choice for crusted scabies, usually requiring multiple doses combined with permethrin. Malathion 0.5% lotion is an alternative topical agent used when permethrin is unavailable. Benzyl benzoate 25% lotion is widely used in low-income settings because of its low cost. All household members and sexual contacts in the preceding four weeks should be treated simultaneously on the same day as the index case regardless of symptoms, since a newly infested person can transmit without yet being symptomatic. Bedding, clothing, and towels used in the 72 hours before treatment should be machine-washed at 50°C or sealed in a bag for 72 hours. Post-scabetic pruritus — itching persisting for up to six weeks after successful treatment — is managed with oral antihistamines and moderate-potency topical corticosteroids; it does not indicate treatment failure. Secondary bacterial infection is treated with appropriate antibiotics, and in some endemic regions empirical antibiotic cover for group A streptococcal impetigo accompanies routine scabies treatment to reduce the serious complication of glomerulonephritis.

medicalTreatments.name: Permethrin 5% cream
medicalTreatments.description: A synthetic pyrethroid applied from neck to soles (including face and scalp in young children) for 8–12 hours, washed off, and repeated at day 7–9; the standard first-line treatment in NICE, CDC, and WHO guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 90–95% with a two-application regimen in randomised trials; considered the most effective topical scabicide currently available.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral ivermectin
medicalTreatments.description: A macrocyclic lactone given at 200 mcg/kg orally as a single dose, repeated at day 8–15; first-line for crusted scabies (multiple doses needed), preferred for mass treatment, and equivalent to permethrin for classic scabies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 85–95% with two doses in randomised controlled trials; highly effective and well tolerated; not licensed for use in pregnancy or children under 15 kg.

medicalTreatments.name: Benzyl benzoate 25% lotion
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to the whole body below the neck (diluted to 12.5% for children) once or twice and repeated after 24 hours; widely used in low-income tropical settings because of its low cost and availability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates reported at approximately 70–80% in clinical studies, somewhat lower than permethrin, but adequate for population-level control programmes.

medicalTreatments.name: Malathion 0.5% aqueous lotion
medicalTreatments.description: An organophosphate agent applied from neck to soles for 24 hours before washing; used as a second-line alternative when permethrin is contraindicated or unavailable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to permethrin in clinical studies, with cure rates in the range of 85–92%; less commonly used as first-line due to the prolonged application time and smell.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic therapy for secondary bacterial infection
medicalTreatments.description: Oral flucloxacillin or cephalexin for staphylococcal superinfection; phenoxymethylpenicillin or amoxicillin for streptococcal impetigo; systemic antibiotics chosen based on local resistance patterns and wound swab results.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bacterial secondary infections resolve in 5–7 days with an appropriate antibiotic; in high-endemic settings, empirical treatment can reduce the group A streptococcal complication rate of acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Crotamiton 10% cream
alternativeTreatments.description: An older scabicidal and antipruritic agent applied nightly for five nights; lower cure rates than permethrin (approximately 50–60%) but occasionally used for children or when first-line agents are unavailable.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sulfur 5–10% in petrolatum
alternativeTreatments.description: The oldest scabicide still in use in resource-limited settings; applied nightly for three nights and washed off; less cosmetically acceptable but safe in pregnancy and infants under two months when permethrin is cautioned.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tea-tree oil preparations
alternativeTreatments.description: Laboratory studies show acaricidal activity but controlled clinical trial evidence in human scabies is insufficient to recommend as a standalone treatment; not substituted for approved agents.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist or infectious disease specialist should be involved when the diagnosis is uncertain, when treatment has failed after two correctly applied permethrin courses, or when crusted scabies is suspected. Crusted scabies requires aggressive combined oral and topical treatment, infection control measures, and simultaneous treatment of all close contacts and healthcare staff. Outbreaks in care homes or hospitals require a public health response.
doctorSelectionGuide: Most uncomplicated scabies is managed well in primary care or by a general practitioner with experience in skin conditions. For refractory, crusted, or outbreak-related scabies, a dermatologist with expertise in parasitic skin diseases and familiarity with combined oral and topical regimens is appropriate. Sexual health clinic services are valuable when the transmission route is sexual, as they include partner notification and STI co-screening.
preventionStrategies: Treat all household members and sexual contacts on the same day as the index case, regardless of whether they have symptoms yet.
Wash all bedding, clothing, and towels used in the past 72 hours at 50°C or above and dry on a hot tumble-dry cycle.
Seal unwashable items in a sealed plastic bag for at least 72 hours.
Avoid sharing towels, bedclothes, or clothing with any person known or suspected to have scabies.
In care home and institutional settings, implement contact precautions and cohorting until all residents and staff have been treated.
For crusted scabies, apply contact precautions (gloves and gowns for carers) and ensure environmental decontamination of the room and bedding.
In high-prevalence communities, mass drug administration campaigns with single-dose oral ivermectin can reduce community transmission.
lifestyleModifications: Complete both treatment applications on the correct schedule (day 0 and day 7–9); missing the second application is a common reason for treatment failure.
Reapply permethrin to the hands after washing during the treatment period, as hand-washing removes the agent from the most critical infection site.
Manage post-scabetic itch with over-the-counter antihistamines and topical hydrocortisone; do not retreat with scabicide more than twice without clinical assessment.
Avoid sexual contact until you and all partners have completed the full treatment regimen.
In institutional settings, cooperate with infection control staff for coordinated outbreak management.

recommended: No specific dietary changes are required for scabies management
Adequate protein and vitamin C intake supports skin repair in cases with extensive secondary infection or widespread excoriation
avoid: No specific foods are contraindicated during scabies treatment

exerciseGuidelines: No exercise restrictions are required for scabies. During the treatment period, apply permethrin before bedtime to avoid immediate removal by sweating during exercise. Shower before applying the cream if you have been exercising. Avoid sharing exercise equipment, towels, or clothing during an active infestation.
dailyManagement: Apply permethrin to every square centimetre of skin from the neck down, including under nails, in the groin, and between toes — incomplete application is the most common cause of first-treatment failure.
Take oral antihistamines at night during the active and post-treatment phases to reduce pruritus-related sleep disruption.
Inform sexual contacts and household members promptly so they can be treated on the same day.
If itching persists beyond four to six weeks after completing two treatment courses, return for clinical assessment to distinguish post-scabetic pruritus from re-infestation.
Monitor for signs of secondary bacterial infection — spreading redness, pus, fever — and seek same-day care if they develop.
prognosis: The prognosis for classic scabies is excellent. With a correctly applied two-dose permethrin regimen and simultaneous treatment of all household contacts, cure rates approach 90–95%. The main cause of apparent treatment failure is re-infestation from an untreated contact rather than true drug resistance. Post-scabetic pruritus, which affects most treated patients, typically resolves within four to six weeks and is not a marker of ongoing infestation. Crusted scabies carries a more protracted course requiring repeated combined oral and topical treatment over two to three weeks, and patients may require hospitalisation. The major long-term consequences of scabies occur in high-burden settings where secondary bacterial infection with Streptococcus pyogenes leads to impetigo, cellulitis, and, most seriously, acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis and rheumatic fever — conditions that contribute meaningfully to the global burden of kidney and heart disease in low-income tropical regions.
recoveryTimeline: After correct treatment, living mites are killed within 24 hours of permethrin application. Itching typically begins to improve within two to three days of the first treatment but commonly persists for two to six weeks as dead mite antigens clear from the skin. The second treatment course at day 7–9 addresses any larvae that were in the egg stage and survived the first application. New lesions should not appear after the second treatment; any new burrows or papules appearing more than two weeks after treatment suggest re-infestation. Secondary bacterial infections resolve over five to seven days with appropriate antibiotic treatment.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection with Streptococcus pyogenes or Staphylococcus aureus causing impetigo, folliculitis, or cellulitis, the most common complication, especially in children in tropical settings.

Acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis following streptococcal superinfection of scabies lesions — a serious renal complication causing haematuria, proteinuria, and hypertension, predominantly in children in low-income regions.
Crusted scabies in immunocompromised patients, characterised by a hyperinfestation with millions of mites, extreme contagiousness, and the need for aggressive combined treatment.
Post-scabetic nodules — persistent itchy inflammatory nodules in the groin or axillae lasting weeks to months after mite eradication, representing a prolonged hypersensitivity response.
Widespread eczematisation and excoriation creating a chronic dermatitis-like picture that can be difficult to distinguish from primary eczema, particularly in infants.
Psychological impact of a stigmatised skin condition — anxiety, social withdrawal, and stigma-related delay in treatment-seeking, which perpetuates household transmission.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema produces chronic pruritic dermatitis in atopic individuals without visible burrows, mites, or household clusters of itching; scabies produces a characteristic burrow distribution and affects multiple household contacts simultaneously.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis produces well-defined scaly plaques at extensor surfaces and is a chronic immune-mediated condition without the nocturnal itch, burrows, or household transmission pattern of scabies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Phthiriasis (pubic lice)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pubic lice are visible on hair shafts, cause pruritus limited to hair-bearing areas, and show lice and nits rather than burrows; scabies causes burrows in hairless areas such as finger webs and is caused by a burrowing mite.

relatedConditions.name: Phthiriasis (pubic lice)
relatedConditions.relevance: Another ectoparasite infestation transmitted through close skin contact, often diagnosed in the same sexual health setting as scabies and requiring similar simultaneous partner treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common differential for scabies; post-scabetic dermatitis can mimic eczema, and pre-existing eczema can mask the typical scabies rash.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhoea
relatedConditions.relevance: Scabies acquired sexually is associated with concurrent bacterial STIs; gonorrhoea is included in standard co-infection screening when scabies is diagnosed via the sexual contact route.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Herpes simplex is a frequent co-infection screened for when scabies is diagnosed through a sexual health service.

faqs.question: What is scabies?
faqs.answer: Scabies is a skin infestation by the microscopic mite Sarcoptes scabiei, which burrows into the outer skin layer to lay eggs. The immune system's reaction to mite proteins causes intense itching, worst at night, and a rash of papules and burrows.

faqs.question: How do you catch scabies?
faqs.answer: Scabies spreads through prolonged skin-to-skin contact with an infested person — as occurs during sexual contact, sleeping in the same bed, or caring for someone with scabies. The mite cannot jump or fly. Sharing infested bedding or clothing is a secondary transmission route.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of scabies?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are intense itching that is much worse at night, a rash of small red bumps and blisters, and thin thread-like burrow tracks in the skin most often found between the fingers, on the wrists, and on the genitals. Several people in the same household often develop symptoms around the same time.

faqs.question: How is scabies treated?
faqs.answer: Apply permethrin 5% cream from the neck to the soles of the feet, leave it on for 8–12 hours, wash off, and repeat seven to nine days later. All household members must be treated on the same day. Wash all bedding and clothing at 50°C. Oral ivermectin is an equally effective alternative.

faqs.question: How long does scabies last without treatment?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, scabies persists indefinitely because the mite continues to reproduce. The infestation does not go away on its own in the vast majority of cases, and itching worsens progressively. Prompt treatment is needed to break the cycle.

faqs.question: What do scabies burrows look like?
faqs.answer: Burrows appear as thin, wavy, grey-white lines 5–15 mm long on the skin surface, most reliably found between the fingers, on the inner wrists, and on the genitals. They represent the tunnel the female mite has excavated. A dark dot — the mite — may be visible at one end under a magnifying glass.

faqs.question: Can scabies go away on its own?
faqs.answer: In virtually all cases, scabies does not resolve without treatment. The mite population may fluctuate but the infestation is self-sustaining. Waiting without treatment prolongs suffering and allows onward spread to household contacts.

faqs.question: Why does scabies itch more at night?
faqs.answer: Nocturnal pruritus is a classic feature of scabies because the mites are more active in warm skin conditions, and because the body's natural histamine release and sensitisation response peak at night. The nighttime itch that disrupts sleep is one of the most reliable diagnostic clues.

faqs.question: Do I have to treat my whole family?
faqs.answer: Yes — all household members and recent sexual partners must be treated on the same day as you, even if they have no symptoms yet. A newly infested person can transmit scabies before symptoms start. Treating only the symptomatic person and not the household virtually guarantees reinfection.

faqs.question: Why does itching continue after treatment?
faqs.answer: Post-scabetic pruritus — itching that persists for two to six weeks after successful treatment — is caused by your immune system reacting to dead mite proteins still in the skin. It is not a sign that the treatment failed or that live mites are still present. Antihistamines and mild topical steroid cream relieve the itch while the reaction settles.

faqs.question: What is crusted scabies?
faqs.answer: Crusted scabies — also called Norwegian scabies — is a severe form in which millions of mites colonise the skin of an immunocompromised or elderly person, producing thick grey crusts over the body. It is extremely contagious and requires aggressive combined oral and topical treatment as well as isolation in healthcare settings.

faqs.question: Can scabies spread through a swimming pool?
faqs.answer: Scabies transmission in a swimming pool is considered unlikely because the mite requires prolonged direct skin-to-skin contact of several minutes and does not survive well in chlorinated water. The pool environment itself is not considered a significant transmission route.

faqs.question: How do I know if the scabies treatment worked?
faqs.answer: After completing both applications of permethrin, new burrows or papules should not appear. Itching may continue for up to six weeks even after a cure. If you develop new burrows — not just persistent itch — more than two weeks after completing both treatment courses, you have likely been reinfested from an untreated contact.

faqs.question: Can scabies affect the face?
faqs.answer: In adults, scabies typically spares the face and scalp. In infants and children under two, the face, scalp, and neck are commonly affected and should be included in the treatment application area. Crusted scabies can involve the face in severely immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: Do I need to fumigate my house if I have scabies?
faqs.answer: Fumigation is unnecessary for classic scabies because the mite survives off the host for only 24–36 hours. Machine-washing bedding and clothing at 50°C and vacuuming mattresses and upholstered furniture is sufficient. Fumigation may be considered in environments with crusted scabies because the vast number of shed mites creates a greater environmental reservoir.

faqs.question: Can you get scabies from a toilet seat?
faqs.answer: Getting scabies from a toilet seat is extremely unlikely. The mite cannot survive long on hard surfaces and does not transfer efficiently from cold non-skin contact. The dominant route is prolonged warm skin-to-skin contact of several minutes or more.

faqs.question: Is scabies an STI?
faqs.answer: Scabies can be transmitted through sexual activity because of the prolonged skin contact involved, and it is routinely screened for in sexual health clinics alongside bacterial STIs. However, it also spreads through non-sexual close contact within households and care settings, so it is more accurately described as an ectoparasite infestation than a purely sexually transmitted infection.

faqs.question: Can scabies cause permanent skin damage?
faqs.answer: Classic scabies does not cause permanent skin damage once the mites are eliminated and the inflammatory reaction resolves. Secondary bacterial infection that goes untreated for a prolonged period can scar, and crusted scabies in an immunocompromised patient may lead to more extensive skin changes, but full skin recovery is typical with prompt management.

faqs.question: Is permethrin cream safe for children?
faqs.answer: Permethrin 5% cream is considered safe for infants from two months of age and children of all ages, though the application area should include the head and face for children under two years. Parents should avoid contact of the cream with the eyes and mouth and wash it off after the prescribed contact time.

faqs.question: What complications can scabies cause?
faqs.answer: Secondary bacterial skin infection — impetigo, cellulitis, or folliculitis — is the most common complication. In tropical regions, streptococcal infection from scabies lesions can lead to acute glomerulonephritis. Crusted scabies in immunocompromised patients can be a serious systemic illness. Psychological stigma and sleep deprivation from the itching contribute significantly to quality-of-life impact.

metaDescription: Scabies is a mite infestation causing intense nocturnal itching and burrows in the skin. Learn symptoms, permethrin treatment and household management.
